title: Why some survivors cannot tolerate being touched
author: Kalesh

Your body is not a door that anyone can open

htmlContent:
Your body is not a door that anyone can open

I remember sitting on my couch, my partner's hand reaching for my shoulder, and feeling my entire nervous system scream NO before my brain could even form the thought. My body flinched. My chest tightened. And then came the shame, the confusion, the inner voice asking what is wrong with me.

Nothing was wrong with me. I was surviving.

If you've ever felt that visceral recoil when someone tries to touch you, even someone you love, even someone you trust, you know the isolation that comes with it. You watch other people hug freely, touch casually, each other without a second thought. And you wonder why your body treats contact like a threat.

Here's the thing. You're not broken. Your nervous system is doing exactly what it learned to do.

Touch aversion in survivors of complex trauma is not a character flaw, not a sign that you're incapable of intimacy, not a permanent sentence. It's a survival adaptation that once kept you alive. And understanding that changes everything.

The nervous system's memory of touch

Let me tell you about Stephen Porges. He's the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, and his work explains so much about why touch can feel unbearable for trauma survivors. Porges showed that our nervous system is constantly scanning for safety and danger, something he calls neuroception. This happens below conscious awareness, faster than thought.

Your body doesn't ask your permission before deciding whether a touch is safe or threatening. It just reacts. And if your history includes touch that was violating, painful, or unpredictable, your nervous system learned that touch equals danger. It's not being dramatic. It's being accurate based on your past.

Think about it. When someone reaches for you, your body runs a split-second assessment. Is this person safe? Is their hand moving in a way that matches past threat? Is my system prepared to defend itself? For survivors, the answer is often no before the fingers even make contact.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body, not just the mind. Touch doesn't just trigger a memory of what happened. It triggers the actual physiological state you were in during the trauma. Your muscles tense. Your breath shortens. Your heart races. You're not remembering the fear. You're reliving it, right there in your skin.

That's why no amount of telling yourself "this person is safe" makes the reaction stop. Your rational brain doesn't run the show when your nervous system is screaming danger. Your body has its own intelligence, and it's trying to protect you.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate. Magnesium helps regulate the nervous system and can reduce that hypervigilant edge that makes touch feel overwhelming. It's not a cure, but it can take the volume down enough to start working with your body instead of against it.

The layers of touch aversion

Touch aversion isn't one thing. It's a spectrum, and it shows up differently depending on your history, your current state, and who's touching you.

Some survivors can't tolerate any touch at all. Not from strangers, not from friends, not from partners. Even a casual brush in a crowded store can send them into a freeze response. Others are fine with certain types of touch but not others. Maybe you can handle a handshake but not a hug. Maybe you love deep pressure like a weighted blanket but can't stand light, unpredictable touches.

Stay with me here. This is where it gets specific.

Janina Fisher, a trauma specialist who integrates parts work and somatic approaches, describes how different "parts" of us can have different relationships with touch. One part of you might desperately want connection. Another part might be convinced that touch will destroy you. Both are valid. Both are trying to help in their own way.

The problem isn't that you have these conflicting responses. The problem is that we've been told touch is supposed to feel good, that intimacy requires physical closeness, that something is wrong with us if we don't want to be held. That's a lie. Or at least, it's an incomplete truth.

For many survivors, touch aversion is layered with shame. You feel broken because you can't do something that seems so simple for everyone else. You push yourself to tolerate touch you don't want, which only makes your nervous system more reactive. You end up dissociating during contact, which leaves you feeling even more disconnected from yourself and your partner.

Here's what nobody tells you. The goal is not to force your body to tolerate touch it doesn't want. The goal is to rebuild a relationship with your body where touch becomes possible because it's chosen, not endured.

I've found that working with my nervous system in small, manageable steps is the only thing that actually helps. A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs. The deep pressure stimulation can mimic the feeling of being held without the unpredictability of another person's touch. It's a way to practice feeling safe in your skin on your own terms.

Why "just relax" makes it worse

I need to be direct about something. The wellness industry has done a lot of damage to trauma survivors with its insistence that all you need is to breathe, to let go, to surrender to love. That advice assumes your body is starting from a place of safety. It's not.

When someone tells you to just relax and accept touch, they're asking you to override your survival instincts. That's not healing. That's re-enactment. You've already spent years ignoring what your body was telling you. More of that won't help.

Pat Ogden, who developed Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, emphasizes that the body needs to be met where it is, not where we want it to be. If your body says no to touch, the most healing thing you can do is listen. Not argue. Not negotiate. Not shame yourself into compliance. Just listen.

That might sound like giving up. It's actually the opposite. It's the beginning of real trust between you and your body.

When you stop forcing yourself to tolerate touch you don't want, something shifts. Your nervous system starts to notice that you're paying attention. That you're on its side. That you're not going to override its warnings anymore. And slowly, over time, the warnings start to soften. Not because you fought them, but because you honored them.

Notice what just happened in your body when you read that. Did something relax? Did something tense up? That's your system responding to the possibility of being heard.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger by Peter Levine. It walks through how trauma gets trapped in the body and how to gently release it without having to relive the worst moments. It helped me understand why my body was so reactive and what I could actually do about it.

Rebuilding touch from the inside out

So what does it actually look like to heal touch aversion? It's not linear. It's not pretty. And it looks different for everyone.

For me, it started with noticing. I spent weeks just paying attention to what my body felt like when I was alone, when I was safe, when no one was going to touch me. I learned the difference between a relaxed muscle and a guarded one. I learned where I held tension. I learned that my shoulders were practically touching my ears most of the time.

Then I started experimenting with self-touch. Not in a sexual way. Just putting my own hand on my own arm and noticing what happened. Most of the time, nothing good. My body recoiled from my own touch. That was hard to face. But it was honest.

I kept going. I used a soft blanket against my skin. I put pressure on my own feet. I found that certain kinds of touch felt okay, even nice, when I was the one controlling them. That was a revelation. It wasn't that touch was bad. It was that I needed to be in charge.

This is where things get practical. If you're working with touch aversion, here are some things that might help, but only if they feel right for you. No pressure. No shoulds.

Start with your own body. Learn what your touch feels like. Can you put your hand on your chest without tensing? Can you stroke your own arm? If not, that's information, not failure.

Use tools that give you control. A discreet option for reducing noise sensitivity is Loop Quiet 2 Ear Plugs. They lower sensory input without blocking it completely, which can make your nervous system feel safe enough to explore touch at your own pace. When your ears aren't overwhelmed, your body can sometimes tolerate more.

Communicate with people in your life. This is the hardest part, I know. But telling someone "I need to be the one who initiates touch right now" is a legitimate boundary. You don't have to explain your whole history. You just have to state what you need.

Work with a therapist who understands trauma and the body. Not all therapists do. Look for someone trained in Somatic Experiencing, Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, or a similar approach. Talk therapy alone won't reach the body's memory of touch.

And please, stop measuring your progress by whether you can tolerate touch you don't want. Measure it by whether you can honor what your body tells you. Measure it by whether you can stay present with yourself even when touch isn't possible yet. That's the real work.
